query: evidence that differentiates acute vs chronic kidney injury based on biomarkers

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Biopsy -- Indications -- Non-Targeted Biopsies for Differentiating Causes of Acute Kidney Injury or Chronic Kidney Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Non-targeted biopsies are performed to differentiate etiologies of acute kidney injury or chronic kidney disease in patients with the potential for recoverable renal function, as the treatment approach may vary depending on the underlying etiology.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Biopsy -- Indications -- Non-Targeted Biopsies for Differentiating Causes of Acute Kidney Injury or Chronic Kidney Disease
retrieved_docs.content: For non-targeted biopsies, neither the American Society of Nephrology nor the National Kidney Foundation provide guidelines in any general or specific setting, such as acute kidney injury, chronic kidney disease, or post-transplant care. [15] These guidelines do not address many of the specific questions or issues encountered in everyday medical practice pertaining to the use of specific imaging or clinical parameters to differentiate types of diffuse parenchymal renal diseases with high positive predictive value.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Function Tests -- Procedures -- Acute Versus Chronic Renal Impairment
retrieved_docs.content: Urine output is a useful tool for evaluating kidney function and is used in guidelines to define acute kidney injury. Patients with acute kidney injury present with oliguria, defined as less than 400 mL/d or less than 0.5 mL/kg/h for more than 6 hours. [27] The RIFLE classification (risk, injury, failure, loss of kidney function, and end-stage kidney disease) is based on serum creatinine, GFR changes, and urine output determinants. The Acute Kidney Injury Network (AKIN) classification criteria for acute kidney injury also uses serum creatinine changes and urine output; however, it does not rely on GFR changes and does not require a baseline serum creatinine.

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation and Management of Perioperative Hypertension -- Complications -- Acute Kidney Injury
retrieved_docs.content: The literature contains numerous definitions of kidney injury. In 2004, the RIFLE classification was proposed to distinguish between different levels of kidney injury and to facilitate comparison of studies of AKI. [44] It has been shown useful to predict outcomes. One limitation of the RIFLE criteria is the requirement to know baseline serum creatinine (sCr), a value that is sometimes unavailable. Additional limitations of the RIFLE criteria have been described. [44] The Acute Kidney Injury Network (AKIN) classification was first reported in 2007. It is a modification of the RIFLE criteria. The advantages of the AKIN classification are that it can be used when baseline sCr is unknown and has been shown to increase the identification of patients with AKI as compared with RIFLE. [44] A weakness of both RIFLE and AKIN is their use of sCr, which may take several days to rise after renal insult. In contrast, urinary and serum biomarkers now exist that rise one to three days before sCr. [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Kidney Injury -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Although creatinine is the most commonly used serum chemistry to evaluate for AKI, there is evidence that other biomarkers may be more sensitive and may be elevated earlier in the course of AKI compared to creatinine. Some of these include neutrophil gelatinase–associated lipocalin (NGAL), which can be measured in the plasma and urine, kidney injury binding protein-1 (KIM-1), retinol-binding protein, cystatin C, α/β microglobulin, and urine uromodulin. The use of these biomarkers is not widely available and has not yet been validated in wide-scale studies. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Biopsy -- Indications -- Non-Targeted Biopsies for Differentiating Causes of Acute Kidney Injury or Chronic Kidney Disease
retrieved_docs.content: The 2024 American College of Rheumatology (ACR) guidelines (available on the society's website) recommend performing kidney biopsy in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus who have high protein levels in the urine or impaired kidney function that is not otherwise explained. The 2023 joint ACR-SIR guidelines (available on the ACR website) state only that non-targeted biopsies are appropriate for determining the nature and extent of diffuse parenchymal diseases, such as renal transplant rejection and glomerulonephritis. The 2013 ACR guidelines indicate that, for acute kidney injury, a biopsy is the next most appropriate radiologic test after performing an ultrasound of both the kidneys and bladder. For chronic kidney disease, the ultrasound of the kidneys and bladder should be performed first, but a biopsy is of higher or equivalent utility compared to any other radiologic test. [14] The 2020 updated version of these guidelines omitted this commentary.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Renal Tubular Necrosis -- Evaluation -- Novel biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Numerous biomarkers have evolved to detect AKI/acute tubular necrosis earlier than serum creatinine. These biomarkers include serum cystatin C as an early and reliable marker of renal injury compared to a rise in serum creatinine, which is often witnessed 48 to 72 hours after the initial insult. [10] [18] Other markers include urinary α-one microglobulin, β-2 microglobulin, hemoxygenase-1, osteopontin, and kidney injury molecule 1. [10] [16] Urinary biomarker neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin is reliably upregulated in renal ischemia after tubular injury and is one of the best-studied markers for kidney injury; it is considered sensitive and specific and begins to rise 2–6 hours after initial kidney injury, significantly earlier than creatinine. [37] [38] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor–Associated Acute Kidney Injury -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Several research studies are currently underway to identify noninvasive markers that can differentiate ICI-AKI from non–ICI-AKI. Urinary biomarkers such as tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), CXCL-9, retinol-binding protein, and PD-1 have been proposed as potential indicators but require validation on a larger scale. [33] Soluble IL-2 receptor (a blood-based biomarker) has been shown to be elevated in the peripheral blood of patients with ICI-AIN compared to hemodynamic AKI controls, as well as ICI-treated patients without AKI. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Contrast-Induced Nephropathy -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Assessment of acute kidney injury with serum creatinine has poor sensitivity and specificity. Several new markers have been identified with most of the current interest focused on cystatin C, neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin, interleukin 18, and kidney injury molecule 1.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Kidney Injury -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: There are markers of tubular function that can be calculated to help distinguish between prerenal, intrarenal, and postrenal causes, such as the fractional excretion of sodium and urea and urine osmolality; however, these parameters are affected by many drugs commonly used in clinical practice such as diuretics and are not always diagnostic. No single marker can be reliably used in isolation to distinguish among the causes of AKI, and clinical presentation must be considered.

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Acute Kidney Injury -- Introduction -- Kidney Disease Improving Global Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The identification of AKI is now possible using several novel biomarkers, even at values that do not meet the conventional diagnostic criteria, a condition referred to as "subclinical AKI." Some of these markers represent structural damage to the kidney which may or may not affect its filtration capacity. [6] [7] Traditional criteria, such as plasma creatinine level, urine output, and less commonly cystatin C, measure the kidney's filtration ability rather than structural damage and, as such, can be labeled as "functional AKI." [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Kidney Injury -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Over the long term, at least 12% to 15% of patients with AKI may require permanent dialysis. Mortality is increased in patients with high APACHE III scores, advanced age, and persistent creatinine elevation. [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Biopsy -- Indications -- Non-Targeted Biopsies for Differentiating Causes of Acute Kidney Injury or Chronic Kidney Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Fiorentino [16] created an evidence-based list of indications and conclusions, and Fuiano [17] conducted a worldwide survey to obtain opinions on indications for non-targeted biopsies. The specific relative indications for non-targeted biopsies described by Fiorentino and Fuiano—including various types of nephrotic syndrome and glomerulonephritis—are extensive. Readers are encouraged to refer to their papers, the National Kidney Foundation guidelines, [15] or numerous other available non-consensus guideline-based sources with suggested indications for non-targeted biopsies.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Function Tests -- Procedures -- Novel Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Several new biomarkers have been reported to be useful for determining acute kidney injury and have utility in differentiation between acute kidney injury and stable CKD and pre-renal and intrinsic acute kidney injury. These biomarkers include low-molecular-weight proteins and fall into 2 main categories as follows: Proteins and chemicals present in the systemic circulation, which undergo glomerular filtration Cystatin C Beta-2-microglobulin Retinol-binding protein Recently recognized filtration markers pseudouridine, acetylthreonine, myoinositol, phenylacetylglutamine, and tryptophan [14] Proteins that are produced in response to cellular or tissue injury Neutrophil gelatinase–associated lipocalin (NGAL) Kidney injury molecule 1 (KIM-1) L-type fatty acid–binding protein (L-FABP) Fibroblast growth factor 23 (FGF23) Beta-trace protein The optimum clinical utility of these substances is further clarified through ongoing research.

retrieved_docs.title: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor–Associated Acute Kidney Injury -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Definitions of partial and complete renal recovery vary across studies; however, renal recovery is overall common in patients with ICI-AKI, occurring in up to 67% of cases. [4] Early corticosteroid use is associated with a greater likelihood of renal recovery, while more severe AKI correlates with lower odds of recovery. [4] [40] Lack of renal recovery is associated with higher mortality compared to partial or complete kidney recovery. [17] Patients who develop AKI are more likely to progress to chronic kidney disease (CKD) and face a higher risk of eGFR decline over time, which may affect eligibility for future therapies. Given the implications of ICI-AKI, including its association with increased mortality, it is essential to diligently monitor and proactively manage AKI when it occurs. [4] [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Acute Kidney Injury -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Innovations in laboratory analysis of AKI have determined the existence of AKI biomarkers that can be detected before renal mass is lost. [9] These biomarkers are surrogates of early stress on the renal tubules. They are mobilized secondary to cardiopulmonary bypass and surgical stimulation and reflect underlying mechanisms of renal injury since different biomarkers are released in response to specific insults. Of note, insults due to G1-phase cell cycle arrest are associated with tissue-inhibitor of metalloproteinase-2 (TIMP-2) and insulin-like growth factor-binding protein-7 (IGFBP-7) biomarkers and are predictive of major adverse kidney events, including death, persistent renal dysfunction, and need for dialysis. [28] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 894
retrieved_docs.content: is identified as a member of الفشل الكلوي الحاد (SMQ), linking inflammatory kidney disease to the same acute kidney failure classification used for hemodialysis‑related conditions. Together, these interconnections highlight how hemodialysis‑related disorders, their sudden‑onset presentations, associated comorbidities, and classification within acute kidney failure frameworks are tightly integrated in medical understanding and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2686
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney injury, acidosis, hyperkalemia, and extrarenal blood nitrogen are closely interrelated. Acute kidney injury is clinically associated with both acidosis and hyperkalemia, indicating that patients with sudden loss of renal function frequently develop metabolic acidosis and elevated serum potassium levels. In addition, acidosis and hyperkalemia commonly co‑occur, reflecting shared pathophysiological mechanisms in renal failure. Acute kidney injury also includes the condition termed extrarenal blood nitrogen, a manifestation of nitrogenous waste accumulation outside the kidneys. Recognizing these linked abnormalities is essential for timely diagnosis and comprehensive management of patients with acute kidney injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 1071

Overall, hemodialysis is a central element of renal therapy that is interconnected with a spectrum of acute disorders, early‑use syndromes, chronic comorbid conditions, and other sudden‑onset pathologies, all of which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1596
retrieved_docs.content: Acute and unspecified renal failure, which is classified as فشل كلوي حاد, is clinically associated with الحماض (acidosis) and فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم (hyperkalemia). The presence of الحماض and فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم frequently co‑occurs, reflecting their linked pathophysiology in renal impairment. Moreover, acute and unspecified renal failure is considered related to, and possibly synonymous with, وكلاء الكليون السفلي (lower urinary tract agents), indicating an overlap in terminology or clinical context between these entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 370
retrieved_docs.content: nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome” group is shown to have acute glomerulonephritis as a parent condition, underscoring the progression from acute inflammatory injury to chronic nephrotic states. Overall, these interconnected classifications emphasize that CKD resides within a network of renal pathologies ranging from acute glomerular inflammation to systemic immune‑mediated diseases such as lupus nephritis and thrombotic microangiopathies like hemolytic uremic syndrome. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in understanding disease progression, overlapping diagnostic categories, and the importance of integrated management across acute and chronic kidney disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1995
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration (التجفاف) is clinically associated with the development of acute kidney injury (فشل كلوي حاد). In turn, acute kidney injury is clinically linked to low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the importance of adequate intravascular volume in preserving renal function. Acute kidney injury also encompasses the condition of extracellular blood nitrogen (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), indicating that disturbances in nitrogenous waste handling outside the kidneys are a relevant component of its clinical spectrum. Understanding these interconnections—how dehydration can precipitate acute kidney injury, how hypovolemia contributes to its progression, and how extracellular nitrogen abnormalities manifest—helps clinicians identify risk factors, monitor patients, and implement timely interventions to protect renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic kidney disease is a broad renal disorder that encompasses a spectrum of conditions including nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome. When elevated blood pressure causes progressive renal damage, the resulting condition is identified as chronic kidney disease due to hypertension, which is a specific subtype of chronic kidney disease. Recognizing that chronic kidney disease can arise from hypertension and that it includes various glomerular diseases such as nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome guides clinicians to prioritize blood‑pressure control and tailored management of the underlying renal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is closely linked to داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي, an anti‑basement membrane disease of the kidney, and it also forms a key component of متلازمة رئية كلوية, a pulmonary‑renal syndrome that involves simultaneous injury to the lungs and kidneys. This interrelationship highlights the importance of recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis not only as a severe renal pathology but also as a manifestation of systemic autoimmune processes that can affect multiple organ systems, guiding comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and coordinated therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 674
retrieved_docs.content: framework that connects renal replacement therapies, pharmacologic agents, etiologic factors (e.g., hypertension, vascular injury, autoimmunity), diverse glomerular and tubular pathologies, and standardized coding structures, thereby supporting accurate diagnosis, coding, and coordinated patient‑centered management in both adult and pediatric nephrology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2503
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a specific, severe form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis) and is directly linked to متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). In the broader context, التهاب كبيبات الكلى is clinically associated with قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic renal failure), indicating that patients with this type of glomerular inflammation are at risk for progressive loss of kidney function. Recognizing the relationship between necrotizing glomerulonephritis, pulmonary‑renal syndrome, and the potential development of chronic renal failure is essential for timely diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: chronic inflammatory small bowel disease and in secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease, underscoring the importance of recognizing a chronic clinical course across diverse organ systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 5 -- Community 2962
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2) and with reduced blood volume (قلة حجم الدم). Type 2 diabetes can predispose patients to acute kidney injury through chronic hyperglycemia‑induced vascular and tubular damage, making the kidneys more vulnerable to hemodynamic stress. Conversely, hypovolemia decreases renal perfusion and can directly precipitate acute kidney failure. Recognizing that both type 2 diabetes and low intravascular volume are important clinical contributors to acute kidney failure guides clinicians to monitor diabetic patients closely, ensure adequate hydration, and intervene promptly to preserve renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a severe, necrotizing form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis), representing a specific subtype within the broader category of glomerular inflammation. This disease is closely associated with متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), a clinical entity in which acute lung involvement—often pulmonary hemorrhage—occurs together with rapid deterioration of renal function. Understanding that necrotizing glomerulonephritis both belongs to the spectrum of glomerulonephritis and can manifest as part of a pulmonary‑renal syndrome is essential for timely diagnosis, comprehensive evaluation of both kidney and lung pathology, and the implementation of appropriate immunosuppressive and supportive therapies to improve patient outcomes.
